title: Zheng He's first treasure voyage sets out
summary: In the autumn of 1405 a Ming imperial fleet of more than 300 ships and some 27,000 men slipped out of the mouth of the Yangzi under the command of the eunuch admiral Zheng He and set course for the ports of Southeast Asia and India. It was the first of seven great treasure voyages, or bao chuan, that would carry the flag of the Yongle emperor across the Indian Ocean over the following three decades, reaching Hormuz, Aden, Mecca, Malindi, and Mombasa. The voyages projected Ming power on a scale not matched by any state before the modern age of European expansion.

sections.heading: Background
sections.body:
The treasure voyages were a project of the Yongle Emperor Zhu Di, third emperor of the Ming dynasty, who had taken the throne in 1402 after a three-year civil war against his nephew the Jianwen Emperor. The rumour that Jianwen had escaped from the burning of the Nanjing palace and might be living abroad is one thread in later explanations of the voyages, but the deeper motives were political and economic: Yongle wanted to demonstrate Ming imperial legitimacy after a contested succession, to bring the rulers of the Indian Ocean rim into the Chinese tributary system, to suppress piracy in the strait of Malacca, and to secure supplies of exotic goods, from spices and precious stones to giraffes, that could adorn the new court he was building at Beijing.

Ming shipbuilding rested on centuries of Song and Yuan tradition. The great Longjiang shipyard at Nanjing had been reactivated on an imperial scale from 1403, along with subsidiary yards in Zhejiang and Fujian. The largest vessels described in the surviving records, the baochuan, or treasure ships, are given dimensions in Ming sources of around 440 feet in length and 180 in beam, figures that would make them the largest wooden ships ever built. Modern maritime historians have debated whether ships so large were engineeringly plausible; recent analyses tend to accept substantially smaller but still very large flagships, perhaps around 200 feet long, alongside a mixed fleet of horse ships, supply ships, warships, and water tankers.

The man chosen to command was Ma He, a Hui Muslim from Yunnan whose father and grandfather had both made the hajj. Captured as a boy when Ming armies conquered Yunnan in the 1380s, he was castrated and taken into service in the imperial household, where he attached himself to the young Prince of Yan. He served Zhu Di through the civil war and, in 1404, received the honorific surname Zheng in recognition of his services. He was in his mid-thirties when the first voyage sailed and a physically striking figure: contemporary accounts describe him as unusually tall, broad, and imposing.

The first fleet is described by the near-contemporary Ming history as consisting of 62 large treasure ships and around 255 supporting vessels, with a total complement of 27,800 men. The soldiers, sailors, envoys, translators, doctors, and Buddhist and Muslim clerics were drawn from all over the empire. They carried silk, porcelain, gold, silver, and copper coin as gifts and trade goods, and a stock of Ming charters ready to be conferred on cooperative rulers. Preparations continued through the spring and summer; departure ceremonies were held at the temple of the Celestial Consort, patron of Chinese sailors, at Liujiagang near modern Taicang. In the autumn of 1405 the fleet moved down the Yangzi, mustered off the Fujian coast at Changle, and, on a favourable monsoon in the early winter, put out to sea.

sections.heading: What happened
sections.body:
The first voyage of 1405 to 1407 followed a route already familiar to Chinese and Muslim merchants for centuries. From Fujian the fleet ran south to Champa on the coast of what is now central Vietnam and then across the South China Sea to Java, at that time divided between the courts of Majapahit and its dependencies. Zheng He then rounded through the Strait of Malacca, calling at Palembang on the east coast of Sumatra, where a large Chinese emigrant community lived under the effective rule of the Cantonese-born pirate leader Chen Zuyi. A first attempt at diplomacy failed; Chen assembled a fleet to attack the Chinese as they came out of harbour on the return leg. Zheng He's warships crushed the ambush, killed some 5,000 pirates, and captured Chen alive. Chen was carried back to Nanjing and executed there in 1407.

From the strait the fleet crossed the Andaman Sea to the Kingdom of Kollam and then continued up the Malabar coast to Calicut, the greatest of the pepper-trading ports of southwest India. The Zamorin of Calicut welcomed the mission; a stone stele in Chinese, Persian, and Tamil was erected commemorating the visit and Ming charters were conferred. Calicut was the natural western turning-point of the first voyage. The fleet loaded pepper, cotton, and precious stones and, in early 1407, caught the returning monsoon back to Sumatra and China. The mission arrived home to a triumphant reception at Nanjing in the autumn of 1407.

The first voyage set the pattern for the six that followed. The second, from 1407 to 1409, again reached Calicut. The third, from 1409 to 1411, culminated in Zheng He's intervention in the succession dispute of Sri Lanka, in which the Sinhalese king Alakeshvara attacked the Chinese fleet at anchor, was defeated in a counter-attack on his own capital, and was taken back to China as a prisoner. He was later returned in exchange for a more cooperative claimant. The fourth, from 1413 to 1415, was the first to cross the Arabian Sea to Hormuz at the mouth of the Persian Gulf. It brought back envoys from more than a dozen states.

The later voyages pushed still further. Detachments of the fifth voyage of 1417 to 1419 and the sixth of 1421 to 1422 called at Aden in Yemen, at Mogadishu and Barawa on the Somali coast, and at Malindi in what is now Kenya. The famous giraffe brought back from Malindi in 1414 or perhaps 1417, presented to Yongle as a symbolic qilin, an auspicious mythical beast, is preserved in a painting attributed to the court artist Shen Du. Another giraffe followed on a later trip. A Chinese-Persian-Tamil trilingual stele from Galle in Sri Lanka, discovered in 1911 and now in the Colombo museum, survives from these missions and gives a rare epigraphic snapshot of Ming diplomacy in the Indian Ocean.

The seventh and last voyage, in 1431 to 1433 under the Xuande Emperor, was the largest and most far-flung. Detachments from Calicut sailed to Hormuz, Aden, Jidda for the hajj, Mogadishu, and Malindi. Zheng He died on the return leg, most probably at sea near Calicut in 1433, and was, according to tradition, buried at sea. A cenotaph outside Nanjing commemorates him.

image.caption: Map of the seven Ming treasure voyages of Zheng He across the Indian Ocean, 1405 to 1433.

sections.heading: Aftermath & consequences
sections.body:
The immediate diplomatic effect of the voyages was striking. Envoys from dozens of Indian Ocean states, from Bengal and Kollam to Aden and Mogadishu, arrived at Nanjing and later Beijing in an unprecedented parade. The Yongle emperor held audiences in the Forbidden City in which giraffes, zebras, ostriches, and lions took their place among the tribute. Ming coins, porcelain, and silks turned up in archaeological contexts along the entire route, and the routes themselves became safer, with the pirate haunts of the strait of Malacca notably reduced.

The cost was enormous. Each voyage required tens of thousands of tons of timber, huge quantities of grain and salted meat, and the mobilisation of shipyards, artisans, and crews in a scale of state expenditure comparable to a major military campaign. When the Yongle emperor died in 1424 on a campaign against the Mongols, court factions hostile to eunuch power and to the fiscal burden of the voyages gained the upper hand under his successor the Hongxi Emperor. Xuande revived the project one last time in 1431 to 1433, but after Zheng He's death the voyages were formally discontinued. In 1436 the Zhengtong Emperor issued an edict forbidding the construction of any more ocean-going ships.

The archives followed. In 1477 the war minister Liu Daxia is said to have burned or hidden most of the records of the voyages in order to prevent the eunuch faction from reviving them; the story is disputed by modern scholars but reflects a wider process by which Ming officialdom deliberately turned its back on ocean exploration. When Portuguese caravels reached the western Indian Ocean at the end of the fifteenth century they found an ocean whose Muslim and Indian networks still flourished but whose Chinese naval presence had disappeared. Ming coastal defences would eventually respond to the piracy of the Wokou raids of the sixteenth century, but with a defensive rather than an expansionary orientation.

The closure of the voyages did not end Chinese participation in Indian Ocean commerce. Private Chinese merchants, often working from Fujian and Guangzhou, continued to trade to Southeast Asia, and Chinese communities from Manila to Malacca grew steadily through the fifteenth and sixteenth centuries. What ended was the state-sponsored projection of naval power on an oceanic scale.

image.caption: Ming court painting of a tribute giraffe brought back from Malindi and presented to the Yongle emperor as a qilin.
image.credit: Chen Zhang, attributed, via Wikimedia Commons

sections.heading: Legacy
sections.body:
Zheng He's voyages have become one of the most discussed episodes of premodern maritime history. For twentieth- and twenty-first-century historians they raise a question the Ming themselves would not have asked in the same way: why did China, having demonstrated the capacity to reach East Africa in the early fifteenth century, not go on to a European-style age of exploration? The answers usually offered, that the voyages were extraordinarily expensive, that they served court display rather than economic policy, that the empire had no need to seek markets in an already prosperous world, and that Confucian-official political culture distrusted eunuch and naval power, are all partial. The larger truth is that the Ming state had different priorities: securing the northern frontier, rebuilding Beijing, and consolidating the domestic order set up by Hongwu.

The voyages also matter to the modern political imagination of Asia. In China Zheng He has been recovered as a heroic figure and cited as a peaceful predecessor to modern Chinese engagement with the Indian Ocean world, including the Belt and Road Initiative. In Southeast Asia, where he is remembered in temples and popular cults from Semarang in Java to Malacca in Malaysia, the memory of the great Muslim admiral has become part of local Chinese diaspora identity. In East Africa the Chinese porcelain sherds occasionally found on the beaches of Kenya are used to tell tourists about the reach of the fifteenth-century fleets.

One popular claim, the argument by the amateur historian Gavin Menzies that a Chinese fleet reached the Americas in 1421, has been overwhelmingly rejected by professional historians as unsupported by the sources. What remains, thoroughly established by Chinese, Arab, Persian, Portuguese, and epigraphic evidence, is impressive enough on its own: a state-run fleet visiting more than thirty countries, exchanging embassies with the Mamluks and the sharifs of Mecca, and returning giraffes to a Chinese emperor delighted with a live qilin. The first voyage in 1405 opened this remarkable window in world maritime history.

worldContext.text: Western Europe in 1405 was in the middle of the Great Schism between rival popes at Rome and Avignon, with Innocent VII at Rome and Benedict XIII at Avignon. In England Henry IV faced Welsh revolt under Owain Glyndwr and northern conspiracy under the Percys; in France the mad king Charles VI's realm was fought over by the Burgundian and Armagnac factions. Portuguese caravels had begun probing down the West African coast but Ceuta lay a decade ahead. In central Europe Bohemia under Wenceslaus IV was restless with the reformist preaching that would produce Jan Hus.

The Turco-Mongol conqueror Timur had died at Otrar in February 1405 while preparing an invasion of Ming China; his empire, stretching from Anatolia to the Punjab, would break up among his sons and grandsons. The Mamluk sultanate of Egypt, under the youthful Faraj, was in fiscal difficulty; Ottoman Anatolia under Mehmed I was recovering from Timur's shattering victory at Ankara in 1402. In Persia Timur's son Shah Rukh would soon establish a brilliant court at Herat.

The Mexica had thrown off Tepanec overlordship a few decades earlier and their king Chimalpopoca ruled in Tenochtitlan; the great Aztec expansion under Itzcoatl and the Triple Alliance still lay a generation ahead. In the central Andes the Inca king Viracocha was preparing the ground for the enormous expansion under his son Pachacuti in 1438. Ancestral Pueblo people occupied dense settlements in the northern Rio Grande valley, and the Mississippian city of Moundville in Alabama was still active.

In Aotearoa the classic Maori period of pa fortifications was well underway. On Rapa Nui moai construction was at or near its peak, though signs of environmental stress on the island were mounting. Polynesian societies across the eastern Pacific continued long-established fishing and horticultural economies. In highland New Guinea and across Melanesia intensive taro and sweet potato agriculture supported dense populations; Aboriginal Australians maintained the fire-managed landscapes of the continent.

In eastern Europe Muscovy under Vasily I was quietly enlarging its lands under Mongol suzerainty; Lithuania and Poland were bound by the Union of Krewo of 1385 and looked toward the great victory over the Teutonic Order at Grunwald in 1410. In the Balkans the Ottoman advance had been temporarily reversed by Timur's victory at Ankara three years earlier, and the sultan Mehmed I was reunifying the empire from Anatolia after the Ottoman civil war.

figures.name: Zheng He (Ma He)
figures.role: Hui Muslim eunuch admiral from Yunnan; commander of all seven Ming treasure voyages between 1405 and 1433
figures.credit: Anonymous later portrait, via Wikimedia Commons

figures.name: The Yongle Emperor
figures.role: Third Ming emperor, sponsor of the treasure voyages, builder of the Forbidden City, and patron of the Yongle Encyclopaedia

figures.name: Ma Huan
figures.role: Chinese Muslim translator who accompanied several voyages; author of the Yingyai Shenglan, the fullest surviving eyewitness account

figures.name: Fei Xin
figures.role: Officer on the fleet whose Xingcha Shenglan preserves further eyewitness detail of the ports of call

figures.name: Chen Zuyi
figures.role: Cantonese-born pirate leader at Palembang defeated on the first voyage and executed at Nanjing in 1407

images.caption: Reconstruction comparing the size of a Ming treasure ship with a Portuguese caravel of the age of Vasco da Gama, based on Ming and later sources.
images.credit: Jan Adkins and Jan Corning, via Wikimedia Commons

images.caption: Tribute Giraffe with Attendant, painting attributed to Shen Du, 1414, showing the qilin-giraffe presented to the Yongle emperor after Zheng He's fourth voyage. Philadelphia Museum of Art.
images.credit: Shen Du (1357 to 1434), via Wikimedia Commons
